evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes bought with real money, micro-transactions
changing their rules after you buy their goods, potentially blocking you, at any time, from using your own property (like in gaming but also 3d-printing).

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, after hearing about this proposition, I couldn't help but hope that this could cover more similar issues. Protecting digital goods can be seen as defending digital property rights to ensure the fairness to the consumer. It is already related to the 2 main points of this act: unfair commercial practices related to dark patterns and addictive design of digital products but could expand the scope of the identified bad practices. At the moment, there is already an EU initiative that succeeded in reaching its country thresholds and voter amount of 1 million signatures, named "Stop Destroying Videogames" that aims at asking to tackle this very issue. In short, this initiative wants to block publishers/developers from destroying (access to) games that already were bought by the consumer. Imagine, if you will, that a fridge company would do the same by selling you a smart fridge that can physically work for 15 years and then after 2 years intentionally release an update locking you out of the fridge because they don't gain enough money (by their liking) by leaving it working. This is, in fact, already illegal by anti planned-obsolescence laws, but this doesn't seem to concern or be enforced with digital goods. I was personally already affected by this: For example, I have a physical copy (CD) of the game "Command & Conquer 4: Tiberian Twilight" that I bought at its release around 2010. However, the game was made in an intentionally predatory way, as it required a permanent connection to the servers of the developer (even to play alone) without alternatives. After a short time, the developer (EA) decided it wasn't worth continuing to maintain servers as it didn't generate more money. This despite there being still a big active community of players at the time. This thus instantly robbed me (and all others) of the possibility of playing the game since then, as no alternative was available. Of course, this was completely preventable as the game is not significantly different from its predecessors, which still work today without issues. As a computer scientist myself, I also can imagine easy solutions to solve this problem so I can assure that this is done in this way with hostile/bad intent. It's not only at the end of its lifetime that the issue appears. Some game companies change a game's content drastically for the worse in order to attract new consumers, not caring about alienating their already existing consumer base for new profit. We should have a way to keep access to the version that we actually bought without compromises in case the up-to-date version isn't an improvement in reality. These are only some of the many predatory practices that big developers put in place, they collude together (directly or indirectly) and form a giant monopoly in practice, crushing the smaller ones. The major issue is that these are big corporations with lots of money, sometimes even subsidized to intentionally keep going with these bad practices to change the market and thus don't care. Sadly, over time, lots of bad practices were/are normalized because nothing is there to prevent them, some examples include: loot boxes bought with real money, micro-transactions, fear of missing out (FOMO) tactics, children addiction manufacturing, left-wing propaganda, audience replacement, bait and switch (=misleading) publicity/changes, pre-sales with false promises, ... . Don't believe "Video Games Europe", it's a lobby group who doesn't seem to want to protect gamers but instead protects the bad practices that appeared over time. The act could also cover companies changing their rules after you buy their goods, potentially blocking you, at any time, from using your own property (like in gaming but also 3d-printing). Malicious software updates could be seen as a dark pattern and thus covered by the Digital Fairness Act as well. Thank you for reading this message. Please note that I am not at all suicidal (safety measure).
